text:
[Paragraph 1]
4. Who Should Be Using Flutter?

[Paragraph 3]
Flutter is suitable for everyone, whether you are working in a company or building apps just for learning or fun. Anyone who wants to develop modern applications should consider using Flutter with Dart for their next project.

[Paragraph 5]
1.4.1 Teams, Project Leads, and CTOs

[Paragraph 7]
Flutter is very useful for companies and development teams because it:

[Paragraph 8]
Increases productivity

[Paragraph 9]
Improves collaboration

[Paragraph 10]
Reduces development effort

[Paragraph 12]
Traditional Problem:

[Paragraph 14]
Before Flutter, companies had to:

[Paragraph 15]
Build separate apps for:

[Paragraph 16]
Web

[Paragraph 17]
iOS

[Paragraph 18]
Android

ragraph 15]
Build separate apps for:

[Paragraph 16]
Web

[Paragraph 17]
iOS

[Paragraph 18]
Android [Paragraph 20]
Each platform needed a different team with different skills

[Paragraph 21]
These teams found it difficult to work together

[Paragraph 23]
How Flutter Solves This:

[Paragraph 25]
With Flutter, one single team can build apps for:

[Paragraph 27]
Web

[Paragraph 28]
iOS

[Paragraph 29]
Android

[Paragraph 31]
All developers use the same tools and skills.

[Paragraph 32]
This makes collaboration easy and fast.

[Paragraph 33]
So, for team leaders and CTOs, Flutter is an ideal choice to reduce cost, time, and effort.

[Paragraph 35]
1.4.2 Individual Developers

[Paragraph 37]
Flutter is also perfect for individual developers.

h 35]
1.4.2 Individual Developers

[Paragraph 37]
Flutter is also perfect for individual developers. [Paragraph 38]
Developers often want to start new projects quickly

[Paragraph 40]
Many times, projects fail due to:

[Paragraph 41]
Complex setup.. Too many tools, SDKs, configurations, and files to manage.

[Paragraph 42]
Long configuration process.. Setting up Android Studio, dependencies, and UI files can take a lot of time in other frameworks.

[Paragraph 44]
Flutter Advantage:

[Paragraph 46]
Flutter has:

[Paragraph 47]
Easy setup

[Paragraph 48]
Fast development

[Paragraph 49]
Quick testing

[Paragraph 51]
If a developer wants to build:

[Paragraph 53]
A prototype

[Paragraph 54]
An MVP (Minimum Viable Product)

[Paragraph 55]
Flutter helps to build and test ideas quickly

ph 54]
An MVP (Minimum Viable Product)

[Paragraph 55]
Flutter helps to build and test ideas quickly [Paragraph 57]
So, Flutter is ideal for developers who want to experiment and build fast.

[Paragraph 59]
1.4.3 Code School Students and Recent CS Graduates

[Paragraph 61]
Today, many students complete coding courses, which means:

[Paragraph 62]
There is high competition for entry-level jobs

[Paragraph 64]
What Makes a Student Stand Out?

[Paragraph 65]
Having a strong portfolio(projects, assignments, achievements)

[Paragraph 66]
Having a published mobile app

[Paragraph 68]
Flutter helps students because:

[Paragraph 69]
One app can work on both Android and iOS

[Paragraph 70]
It is easier than ever to publish real apps

One app can work on both Android and iOS

[Paragraph 70]
It is easier than ever to publish real apps [Paragraph 71]
Students can show real users and real projects in their resume

[Paragraph 72]
So, Flutter is highly recommended for:

[Paragraph 74]
Students

[Paragraph 75]
Fresh computer science graduates

[Paragraph 77]
1.4.4 Open Source Developers

[Paragraph 79]
Flutter and Dart are:

[Paragraph 80]
Open source

[Paragraph 81]
Their:

[Paragraph 82]
Tools

[Paragraph 83]
Libraries

[Paragraph 84]
Frameworks are all open source

[Paragraph 86]
This means:

[Paragraph 88]
Developers can:

[Paragraph 90]
Modify the code

[Paragraph 91]
Contribute to Flutter

[Paragraph 92]
Build their own tools

[Paragraph 94]
So, Flutter is best for open-source contributors.

aragraph 92]
Build their own tools

[Paragraph 94]
So, Flutter is best for open-source contributors. [Paragraph 96]
1.4.5 People Who Value Speed

[Paragraph 98]
Flutter is for people who want:

[Paragraph 100]
Fast development

[Paragraph 101]
Fast app performance

[Paragraph 102]
Fast updates

[Paragraph 104]
Why Flutter is Fast:

[Paragraph 106]
Hot Reload:

[Paragraph 108]
Shows changes instantly

[Paragraph 109]
Quick code writing

[Paragraph 110]
Sub-second rebuild time - The app’s UI can rebuild (refresh) in less than one second.

[Paragraph 112]
Dart Adds More Speed:

[Paragraph 114]
Dart is:

[Paragraph 115]
Strictly typed

[Paragraph 116]
Fully featured

[Paragraph 118]
It helps:

[Paragraph 120]
Reduce errors

[Paragraph 121]
Debug faster

[Paragraph 122]
Avoid repeated problems

[Paragraph 120]
Reduce errors

[Paragraph 121]
Debug faster

[Paragraph 122]
Avoid repeated problems [Paragraph 124]
So, Flutter is best for people who want speed without losing quality.

[Paragraph 126]
1.4.6 People Who Are “Lazy” (Smart Developers)

[Paragraph 128]
Here, “lazy” means smart developers who don’t want to redo solved problems.

[Paragraph 130]
Flutter gives:

[Paragraph 131]
A large collection of ready-made widgets

[Paragraph 132]
Material Design components already built

[Paragraph 133]
Ready solutions for:

[Paragraph 135]
Navigation drawer

[Paragraph 136]
Buttons

[Paragraph 137]
Layouts

[Paragraph 138]
UI components

[Paragraph 140]
So developers:

[Paragraph 142]
Don’t waste time on UI design

[Paragraph 143]
Can focus on business logic

elopers:

[Paragraph 142]
Don’t waste time on UI design

[Paragraph 143]
Can focus on business logic [Paragraph 144]
Can make their app unique

[Paragraph 146]
1.4.7 People Who Value Control

[Paragraph 148]
Flutter also gives full control to developers.

[Paragraph 150]
Every part of the framework is open to customization

[Paragraph 152]
If needed, developers can:

[Paragraph 154]
Write custom rendering

[Paragraph 155]
Control animations

[Paragraph 156]
Modify any layer of the app

[Paragraph 158]
So, Flutter is best for developers who want:

[Paragraph 160]
Full flexibility

[Paragraph 161]
Deep customization

[Paragraph 162]
Complete control over the app

[Paragraph 164]
1.6 Other Mobile Development Options

[Paragraph 162]
Complete control over the app

[Paragraph 164]
1.6 Other Mobile Development Options [Paragraph 166]
Before discussing Flutter as a choice, it is important to understand that developers should always think carefully before selecting any technology. Every tool has different strengths, and no single tool is perfect for all situations. Flutter also is not the correct answer 100% of the time. Therefore, knowing the alternatives helps you decide which method fits your project.

[Paragraph 168]
1.6.1 Native Development (iOS and Android)

[Paragraph 170]
The first option is building native apps for iOS and Android.

[Paragraph 172]
This method provides:

[Paragraph 174]
Maximum control

[Paragraph 175]
Best debugging tools

[Paragraph 176]
Best performance

ragraph 174]
Maximum control

[Paragraph 175]
Best debugging tools

[Paragraph 176]
Best performance [Paragraph 179]
However, the drawback is that you must write everything twice—once for Android and once for iOS.

[Paragraph 180]
In companies, this usually means having separate developers for each platform.

[Paragraph 181]
These teams have different skill sets, and they cannot easily help each other because the codebases are different.

[Paragraph 183]
1.6.2 Cross-Platform JavaScript Options

[Paragraph 185]
The second option is using cross-platform tools that are based on JavaScript, such as:

[Paragraph 187]
Web views

[Paragraph 188]
React Native

[Paragraph 190]
These are not bad choices.

[Paragraph 191]
In fact, they remove many of the problems faced in native development.

bad choices.

[Paragraph 191]
In fact, they remove many of the problems faced in native development. [Paragraph 192]
Most frontend web developers already know JavaScript, so they can easily help create mobile apps.

[Paragraph 193]
This is why companies like Airbnb, Facebook, and Twitter have used React Native for major products.

[Paragraph 194]
However, these approaches also have disadvantages.

[Paragraph 195]
The biggest issue is something called the JavaScript bridge.

[Paragraph 197]
How the JavaScript Bridge Works

[Paragraph 199]
The earliest mobile apps built using cross-platform ideas were simply web pages running inside WebKit (a browser engine).

[Paragraph 200]
These were basically embedded websites.

s running inside WebKit (a browser engine).

[Paragraph 200]
These were basically embedded websites. [Paragraph 201]
The main problem with this approach was that manipulating the DOM was slow, expensive  and not suitable for high-quality mobile experiences.

[Paragraph 202]
Newer platforms improved this by creating a JavaScript bridge.

[Paragraph 203]
The bridge lets JavaScript talk directly to native code.

[Paragraph 204]
Although this solves some issues, it still has limitations.

[Paragraph 206]
Whenever your app needs to communicate with the device’s rendering system, the JavaScript code must be compiled and sent across the bridge to native code.

[Paragraph 207]
In a single interaction, this crossing happens twice:

[Paragraph 209]
1. From the platform to the apps

a single interaction, this crossing happens twice:

[Paragraph 209]
1. From the platform to the apps [Paragraph 210]
2. From the app back to the platform

[Paragraph 212]
Flutter’s Advantage Over JavaScript-Based Tools

[Paragraph 214]
Flutter compiles directly to ARM native code, which is the type of code used by processors in modern phones, wearables, and IoT devices.

[Paragraph 215]
Flutter also has its own rendering engine.

[Paragraph 217]
This means:

[Paragraph 219]
The app runs natively

[Paragraph 220]
It does not need to cross any JavaScript bridge

[Paragraph 221]
It communicates directly with the device

[Paragraph 222]
It controls every pixel on the screen

[Paragraph 224]
Because there is no bridge, debugging is easier, and interactions are faster.

creen

[Paragraph 224]
Because there is no bridge, debugging is easier, and interactions are faster. [Paragraph 226]
1.7 The Immediate Benefits of Flutter

[Paragraph 228]
Developers often feel skeptical about new technologies.

[Paragraph 229]
Their concerns are valid:

[Paragraph 231]
New technologies may change quickly

[Paragraph 232]
They may lack features

[Paragraph 233]
They may not be widely used

[Paragraph 234]
Companies may abandon them

[Paragraph 236]
However, Flutter avoids many of these problems.

[Paragraph 238]
1.7.1 No JavaScript Bridge

[Paragraph 240]
The JavaScript bridge, which is used in most cross-platform frameworks, is a major bottleneck.

[Paragraph 241]
It reduces performance and makes debugging difficult.

works, is a major bottleneck.

[Paragraph 241]
It reduces performance and makes debugging difficult. [Paragraph 242]
Scrolling and animations may not feel smooth.

[Paragraph 243]
Flutter avoids this completely.

[Paragraph 244]
It compiles to native code and uses the Skia engine (the same one used by Chrome) to render.

[Paragraph 245]
Therefore, Dart code does not need runtime translation.

[Paragraph 246]
This gives excellent performance and productivity.

[Paragraph 248]
1.7.2 Compile Time

[Paragraph 250]
Developers coming from native development—especially iOS—know how slow compile times can be.

[Paragraph 252]
Flutter’s compile times are much faster:

[Paragraph 253]
A full compile usually takes less than 30 seconds

’s compile times are much faster:

[Paragraph 253]
A full compile usually takes less than 30 seconds [Paragraph 254]
Incremental compiles take under a second, thanks to hot reload

[Paragraph 255]
This makes development smoother and faster.

Personalized Learning Using Artificial
Intelligence in Online Platforms
BY
SHADHIYA BEGUM S
III BCA B
U23CA115

•AI studies student learning behavior
•It tracks speed, accuracy, and mistakes
•Content is adjusted for each student
•Learning becomes more personalized
•Students get questions based on their level
•Videos are recommended automatically
•Goal is to improve learning outcomes 
Introduction to AI-Based Personalized Learning
Online learning platforms use Artificial Intelligence (AI)

How the Platform Uses AI for Personalization
•AI collects student activity data
•Monitors quiz scores and answers
•Analyzes time taken to complete tasks
•Identifies weak and strong topics
•Detects common mistakes
•Updates learning path continuously
•Recommends suitable questions
•Suggests related video lessons

AI Supporting Different
Types of LearnersSlow Learners
Provides easier questions and repeated
explanations
Offers extra practice and beginner-friendly
content
Average Learners
Maintains balanced difficulty levels
Reinforces concepts with regular feedback
Fast Learners
Provides advanced questions quickly
Limits repetition but may restrict
exploration

Bias and Limitations of AI
Recommendations
AI depends only on past data
May wrongly label learner ability
Fast learners may feel bored
Slow learners may feel underestimated
Repeated content limits exploration
Creativity may be reduced
Learning paths become fixed
Equal opportunities may be affected

Impact on Learning
OpportunitiesStudents follow AI-decided paths
Less exposure to new topics
Over-personalization reduces choice
Learners may lose curiosity
Dependence on AI increases
Human judgment is missing
Diverse learning styles are ignored
Growth beyond AI limits is difficult

Practical Improvements
and SolutionsAllow students to override AI
suggestions
Provide optional advanced content
Add “Explore Freely” mode
Combine AI with teacher guidance
Update AI models regularly
Give students control over
difficulty
Encourage self-paced exploration
Balance AI support with freedom

AI makes learning personalized based
on student performance
It supports slow, average, and fast
learners differently
AI recommendations can sometimes
limit freedom and create bias
A balance between AI guidance and
student choice is necessary
CONCLUSION
Thank You

Creating and Running a
SampleWinApp2 Windows
Application
Presented by
Shadhiya Begum S
III BCA B
U23CA115
SFM23645

Windows applications provide a graphical
user interface (GUI).
They use windows, buttons, labels and text
boxes.
Examples include Calculator, Notepad and
Paint.
In this project we create a C# Windows Forms
application.

SDI stands for Single Document
Interface.
Only one document window is active at
a time.
Each form opens in its own separate
window.
Example: WordPad is an SDI
application.

This application demonstrates Windows
Forms programming.
It performs two main operations.
Calculate factorial of a number.
Check whether a number is prime.

FirstForm – Main menu form.
factorial – Calculates factorial value.
PrimeNo – Checks if a number is prime.
User selects the operation from the
main form.

Add a Label showing 'This is an SDI
Application'.
Add two RadioButtons.
Create Factorial option.
Check Prime Number option.

Start Button – Opens selected form.
Exit Button – Closes the application.
User selects an option and clicks Start.
Program navigates to factorial or prime
form.

User enters a number.
Program calculates factorial using a
loop.
Example: 5! = 5 × 4 × 3 × 2 × 1 = 120.
Result is displayed in the result textbox.

User enters a number.
Program checks divisibility of the
number.
If divisible by numbers other than 1 and
itself → not prime.
Otherwise it is a prime number.

Start the application.
Main menu (FirstForm) appears.
User selects factorial or prime number.
Corresponding form opens and displays
result.

Demonstrates basic GUI development
in C#.
Shows navigation between multiple
forms.
Implements factorial and prime number
algorithms.
Good example for beginners learning
Windows Forms.
Q u e s t i o n s

CONTENT
1. Introduction
2. Importance of a Resume
3. Steps in Preparing a Resume
4. Personal Information
5. Career Objective
6. Educational Qualifications
7. Skills
8. Projects / Experience
9. Achievements and Certifications
10. Resume Optimization
11. Common Mistakes to Avoid
12. Conclusion

A resume is a professional document that
summarizes a person’s skills, education, and
experience.
It is usually the first document employers review
when selecting candidates.
A well-structured resume helps recruiters
quickly understand a candidate’s qualifications
and abilities.
A clear and well-designed resume increases the
chances of getting shortlisted for job interviews.
INTRODUCTION

IM PORTANCE OF A
RESUM EIM PORTANCE OF A
RESUM E
Creates a first impression for recruiters.
Highlights qualifications and
achievements.
Helps employers shortlist candidates.
Shows professional skills and experience
clearly.

Add personal information
Write a career objective
Mention educational
qualifications
List technical and soft skills
Include projects or work
experience
Add achievements and
certifications
Review and optimize the
resume
STEPS IN PREPARING A
RESUM E

PERSONAL INFORMATION PERSONAL INFORM ATION
Include basic details such as:
Full Name
Phone Number
Email Address
Location
LinkedIn / Portfolio (optional)
Use  a professional email address.

CAREER OBJECTIVE
A short statement describing your career
goals.
Explains how your skills can contribute to the
organization.
Example:
“To obtain a challenging position where I can
apply my technical skills and grow
professionally.”

EDUCATIONAL
QUALIFICATIONS
SALES SALES
This section presents the academic background of the
candidate.
It includes details about the courses studied and
institutions attended.
Information such as institution name, year of completion,
and percentage or CGPA can be mentioned.
This helps employers understand the educational
qualifications and knowledge level of the candidate.

SKILLS
The skills section highlights the abilities and
competencies that are relevant to the job.
It usually includes both technical skills and soft skills.
Technical skills refer to knowledge of programming
languages, software tools, or technologies used in a
particular field.
Soft skills refer to personal qualities such as
communication, teamwork, and problem-solving abilities
that help in working effectively with others.

PROJECTS / EXPERIENCE
SALES SALES
This section includes academic projects, internships, or
any work experience related to the field of study.
It highlights the practical knowledge and skills gained
through real or academic work.
Briefly describe the project title and the main work
done.
Including projects or experience helps employers
understand the candidate’s ability to apply knowledge
in practical situations.

ACHIEVEM ENTS AND
CERTIFICATIONS
This section highlights the accomplishments and
additional qualifications of the candidate.
It includes academic achievements and certifications
from online courses.
Participation in workshops, seminars, or technical
competitions can also be mentioned.
This shows the candidate’s interest in learning and skill
development.

Resume optimization means
improving the resume to make it more
effective and professional.
Use clear headings and bullet points
to present information neatly.
Highlight important skills and
achievements relevant to the job.
Keep the resume short, accurate, and
free from grammar or spelling errors.
RESUM E OPTIM IZATION

COM M ON M ISTAKES TO
AVOID
SALES SALES
Avoid including irrelevant or unnecessary information in
the resume.
Do not write long paragraphs; use clear bullet points
instead.
Ensure the resume is free from spelling and grammar
mistakes.
Use a professional email address and provide correct
information.

CONCLUSION
A resume is an important document for job
seekers.
It helps present skills, education, and
achievements in a clear and organized way.
A well-structured resume creates a positive
impression on employers.
Proper resume building and optimization increase
the chances of getting job opportunites.
THANK YOU
